narration.content: This is a story about a family. Not a perfect family — they argued, they complained, and sometimes they made each other really angry. But it's also a story about faith, about miracles, and about a journey across the world that changed everything. It starts with a father named {{person:lehi:Lehi}}, who lived in the city of {{place:jerusalem:Jerusalem}} about six hundred years before Jesus was born.

Jerusalem was in trouble. The people there had stopped following God, and the whole city had become a pretty dark place. That year, God sent prophets — one after another — to warn everyone: "You need to change, or this city's going to be destroyed." But nobody wanted to hear it. They made fun of the prophets. They threw rocks at them. Some were even killed.

Lehi was different. He actually listened. And it worried him — a lot. So he did what he always did when something weighed heavy on his mind. He found a quiet place, dropped to his knees, and prayed. This wasn't a quick little prayer before dinner. Lehi begged God to help the people he loved.

And then something incredible happened. Right there in front of him, a column of fire appeared, hovering over a rock. The flames were so bright and so close that Lehi could feel them. He saw things in that fire — things that shook him so hard his whole body was trembling. When it was over, he could barely walk. He stumbled home and fell onto his bed, completely worn out.

narration.alt: Lehi kneeling in prayer as a pillar of fire appears on a rock before him

narration.content: But the visions weren't done with him yet. As Lehi lay there on his bed, still shaking, something even more amazing happened. It was like the ceiling just disappeared and he could see straight up into heaven. And what he saw? It took his breath away.

{{person:god-the-father:God}} was sitting on a throne, and all around Him — as far as Lehi could see in every direction — were angels. Thousands and thousands of them, all singing. Then Lehi saw someone coming down from heaven, and this person was so bright that he made the sun look dim. Twelve others followed behind him, glowing like stars. They came all the way down to where Lehi was, and the first one handed him a book.

narration.alt: Lehi's vision of heaven opening — God on His throne surrounded by singing angels

narration.content: Lehi opened the book and started reading. What he found inside was both wonderful and terrible at the same time. The terrible part? Jerusalem really was going to be destroyed. People would die, and many others would be dragged away as prisoners to a faraway place called {{place:babylon:Babylon}}. But there was hope woven through all of it — the book also talked about a {{person:jesus-christ:Messiah}} who'd come one day to save the world.

When the vision finally ended, Lehi's heart was so full he could barely contain it. He cried out to God: "Your works are great and marvelous! Your power and mercy cover the whole earth, and you won't let anyone who comes to you be lost!" He was overwhelmed with joy — real, deep-down, can't-stop-smiling joy — because of everything God had shown him.

But here's the thing about Lehi: he wasn't the kind of person who could keep something like that to himself. He went straight into the crowded streets of Jerusalem and started telling everyone what he'd seen. The city was going to fall. A Savior was coming. People needed to change.

You can probably guess how that went. The people of Jerusalem didn't want to hear any of it. They laughed at him. They called him crazy. And then they got angry — really angry. They wanted to kill him, just like they'd done to other prophets before. But God had bigger plans for Lehi. His story was just getting started.

After Lehi told the people of Jerusalem what he'd seen — and they tried to kill him for it — God came to him again. This time it was in a dream, and the message was simple and urgent: "Take your family and get out. Now. They're coming for you."

So picture this. Lehi had a nice house. He had gold, silver, all kinds of valuable things — stuff he'd spent his whole life working for. His family had owned that land for generations. And God told him to leave it all behind. Not pack it up and bring it along. Just... leave it. Walk away with nothing but some tents and enough food to survive.

And that's exactly what he did. He didn't argue. Didn't ask for more time. He gathered his wife, {{person:sariah:Sariah}}, and their four sons — {{person:laman:Laman}} and {{person:lemuel:Lemuel}} (the two oldest), {{person:sam:Sam}}, and {{person:nephi-son-of-lehi:Nephi}} (the youngest) — loaded up some donkeys with tents and food, and walked away from everything they'd ever known.

narration.alt: Lehi's family leaving Jerusalem at dawn, walking into the wilderness

narration.content: They headed south, following the edge of the {{place:red-sea:Red Sea}} through some of the most empty, rugged wilderness you can imagine. After three days of walking — three long, hot, exhausting days — they finally found a valley with a river running through it. Fresh water. Shade. A place they could actually set up camp.

The first thing Lehi did was build a little altar out of rough stones and thank God for getting them there safely. Then he looked at that river, flowing steady and strong toward the sea, and got an idea. He turned to Laman, his oldest son, and said something like: "I wish you could be like this river — always moving toward what's right, never stopping." Then he turned to Lemuel: "And I wish you could be like this valley — solid, steady, and impossible to move away from doing the right thing."

Now, why would a dad say that to his own kids? Because Laman and Lemuel were miserable. Absolutely miserable. They'd just left behind a nice house, all their stuff, probably their friends — and for what? Because their dad had a dream? They thought Lehi was out of his mind. A "visionary man" is what they called him, and they didn't mean it as a compliment. To them, their father had ruined their lives over what they thought were made-up visions.

narration.alt: Lehi's camp in a desert valley by a river, tents set up near water

narration.content: But Lehi didn't back down. Filled with a power that wasn't entirely his own, he spoke to Laman and Lemuel in a voice that made them literally shake. Whatever he said, however he said it, it was enough. They stopped arguing — at least for the moment.

And then there was Nephi. He was the youngest brother, but there was something different about him. Sure, he was tall and strong for his age — but what really set him apart was this: while his older brothers were busy complaining, Nephi went off by himself and talked to God. He didn't understand everything his dad was going through, but he wanted to. He prayed and asked God to help him believe — and God answered. Something shifted inside Nephi's heart, and from that point on, he trusted his father completely.

Nephi told Sam what had happened, and Sam believed him right away. But when Nephi tried to talk to Laman and Lemuel? They wouldn't listen. It hurt Nephi — really hurt him — and he prayed for them, too.

That's when God spoke to Nephi directly and made him a promise he'd carry for the rest of his life: "Because you've got faith, I'm going to bless you. I'll lead your family to a promised land — a place I've already prepared, better than anywhere else on earth. And one day, you'll be a leader and a teacher to your brothers. But if they turn against me, they'll lose my protection."

It was a lot for a young person to take in. A promised land out there somewhere in the unknown. Older brothers who might turn against him. A father who had given up everything and now lived in a tent in the middle of the wilderness, trusting that God knew where they were going. The journey was just getting started.
